div.div: · puzzle box featuring a chair with screen

Inside the puzzle box is a small chair blocked by a barrier, and several marbles. Goal: get exactly one marble to land on the chair seat, without touching any marble directly. Method: tilt the box to isolate one marble near the barrier. Then sharply tap the bottom of the box — the sudden impact sends that marble bouncing up and over the barrier, landing it in the chair seat. The key is firm, quick tap rather than a gentle tilt. Similar marble-box dexterity puzzles are available as retro novelty items.

The Matrimonial Chair's name is a Victorian joke — finding a spouse (getting to the chair) requires both patience and the occasional unconventional approach (the fillip). The name gave puzzlers an immediate memory hook for the unusual solution method.

A box contains a miniature chair with a screen or barrier and marbles representing characters. The puzzle is to seat exactly one marble in the chair without touching it directly. Solution: isolate the marble, tilt the box slowly, then fillip (flick) the bottom sharply to bounce the marble over the screen and into the chair.

svg.text: Source: Puzzles Old and New (1893) · Heritage Parlor
